text: The video shows a large myoma, approximately twelve centimeters in size, alongside signs of acute cholecystitis in the patient.

The surgeon plans the entry point at Palmer's point, located at the mid-clavicular line just below the costal margin, for laparoscopic access. An epigastric port is placed for telescopic visualization and cholecystectomy, while another port is positioned in the lumbar area.

The surgeon transfers the laparoscopic telescope between the epigastric region and the right hypochondrium, preparing to use the right hand for surgical maneuvers in the right hypochondrium. The left hypochondrium is also identified.
The surgeon positions the camera through the epigastric port for visualization during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon introduces a Veress needle at Palmer's point, located at the mid-clavicular line just below the costal margin, to gain access for the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon initiates pneumoperitoneum, visibly causing the abdomen to distend as gas is introduced into the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon removes the varus needle from the fully distended abdomen and enlarges the incision to 11 mm. A 10 mm optical trocar is then introduced at Palmer's point.

The surgeon examines the abdomen under the vision provided by the epigastric port, which is introduced just left of the falciform ligament. This port will be used for both cholecystectomy and myomectomy procedures, serving as a port for the telescope.
The surgeon removes the telescope from its current position and introduces it into the epigastric port, located just left of the falciform ligament.
All laparoscopic ports are positioned, and the surgeon begins the myomectomy procedure. The telescope is inserted through the epigastric port, revealing some adhesions in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a telescope inserted through the epigastric port to visualize and remove adhesions located around the umbilicus on the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate omental adhesions visible in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses an aspiration needle to inject vasopressin after slowly separating the omentum, which is necessary to proceed with the myomectomy.
The surgeon injects vasopressin diluted with saline into the surgical field, resulting in hydro dissection and transient ischemia. The tissue color visibly changes to a marble white appearance.
The needle is removed after infusing 100 ml of saline mixed with vasopressin. The surgeon prepares to use the myoma screw for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to secure the myoma, followed by making an elliptical incision around the myoma.

The surgeon performs an incision over the uterus to access the intramural myoma located in the thickened anterior wall. The myoma is separated from the uterine muscle, requiring careful dissection to establish a clear surgical plane.
The surgeon uses a myoma rod to slowly separate the myoma from the surrounding uterine tissue, carefully manipulating the structure to achieve separation.
The surgeon uses a solid, blunt myoma rod, which has been self-invented, to manipulate the myoma during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a solid stainless steel myoma rod for enucleation and blunt dissection of the myoma, avoiding the use of suction irrigation or harmonic instruments to prevent bending or breaking.
The surgeon repositions the myoma screw closer to the base of the myoma, facilitating its gradual separation from the uterine wall along a well-defined plane, as the myoma begins to emerge.

The surgeon encounters a soft myoma with fluid in the center, indicating degeneration. The myoma screw struggles to maintain grip due to the softness, causing potential overshooting during manipulation.
The surgeon relocates the myoma screw to the base of the myoma, demonstrating ambidextrous technique with the use of three ports for both myomectomy and cholecystectomy procedures.
The surgeon identifies another myoma, which is planned for removal later in the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the base of the myoma using a harmonic scalpel, avoiding forceful avulsion to prevent opening the cavity. The increased diameter of vessels at the base necessitates precise dissection rather than blunt techniques.

The surgeon removes and relocates the myoma screw to an accessible area, maximizing cranial traction on the myoma. The telescope positioned in the epigastric region provides ample space for maneuvering and applying traction.

The surgeon has completed the removal of the myoma and begins suturing. A suture is introduced through the serosa to keep the tail outside, while continuous suturing is performed on the inner muscle layer.
The surgeon sutures the deep muscle layer, pulling the tail and needle end together to tighten the inner layer before moving outward to the serosa.

The surgeon begins continuous suturing of the serous layer, using the left hand to provide traction and stabilize the uterus while the right hand performs the suturing. The inner muscle layer has already been sutured continuously and tightened.
The surgeon repairs the uterine tissue, ensuring that the edges are inverted and not protruding, while suturing the zero muscular edges of the uterus.
The surgeon takes individual bites with the suture needle to approximate the edges of the tissue, ensuring precise closure despite the distance between the edges.
The surgeon uses a strong number one Y grill suture to tighten the inner muscle layer, pulling the suture to achieve greater tension.

The surgeon adjusts the suturing technique by taking an extra bite to tighten the suture, ensuring the serous layer is securely closed. The process involves returning to a previous step to reinforce the closure.

The surgeon pulls the tail and needle to tighten the inner layer of the suture, using a surgeon's knot to secure it. The needle is removed by cutting the suture near it, followed by tightening the second and third knots.

The surgeon identifies a small subserosal myoma and removes it using a harmonic scalpel. The extracted myoma is placed in the anterior cul-de-sac for safekeeping. The procedure transitions to a cholecystectomy.

The surgeon uses a 45 cm instrument from the left lumbar region to provide traction while a telescope is passed through Palmer's point. The procedure involves a cholecystectomy using an epigastric port and one additional port, with a recommendation for beginners to use two extra ports for ease.

The surgeon uses a left-side instrument in a challenging anatomical position, opting for a three-port technique for the procedure. The camera operator utilizes a 30-degree telescope to provide a clear view of the surgical field.
The light cable is positioned at nine o'clock to improve visibility. The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully stripping down the adhesions to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon identifies the Hartsman pouch and the cystic pedicle below it. The stomach appears distended due to the telescope being positioned at the palmar point, affecting the visual clarity.
The surgeon separates the posterior peritoneum over the cystic pedicle, revealing the lymph node of Lund. The posterior peritoneum is clearly visible during the dissection.
The surgeon forms an anterior window between the cystic duct and the artery, completing the dissection of the cystic pedicle during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies a titanium clip to the cystic duct using an instrument inserted through the epigastric port.
The surgeon creates a posterior window near the cystic duct and artery, with a flat titanium clip visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon places the first clip towards the common bile duct (CBD), the second clip towards the gallbladder, and a third clip three millimeters above the first. The cystic duct is then cut with scissors, and the cystic artery is clipped.

A titanium clip is applied over the cystic artery, followed by coagulation using a harmonic device. The surgeon begins separating the gallbladder by applying anteromedial and anterolateral traction, with the peritoneum being opened first.

The surgeon carefully separates the gallbladder from the liver using a single instrument for traction, pulling the gallbladder towards the appendix to maintain a bloodless dissection along the areolar plane.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed, marking the end of the cholecystectomy procedure.
